result: The morning I learned that trees whisper to each other, I stopped trimming the old oak that leans over my driveway. I had imagined the conversation ending mid-sentence, a green thought chopped in half. Instead, I now park beneath its low arms and let the leaves tap the windshield like polite reminders: we are still talking, you are still listening. Beneath the bark, a fungal internet transmits sugar, warning, memory—everything my Wi-Fi can’t taste. I wonder what they say about me when I forget to water the hydrangeas or when I stand shirtless in the moonlight, talking to the dog as if she were a diary that barks. Probably nothing cruel; plants forgive by continuing to grow toward whatever light survives. Yesterday I pressed my ear to the trunk and heard a slow pulse, forty beats per minute, the tempo of sap in March. It felt like leaning against a sleeping whale. I decided obligations could wait; I stayed until my own heart synced, two mammals—one woody, one worried—sharing the same drowsy drummer. When I finally stepped away, the wind rearranged the upper story, and a single leaf spiraled down, landing on my collar like a green badge that read: trainee apprentice, still learning how to stand still. I wore it indoors, forgot it was there, found it hours later curled like a tiny scroll on the kitchen table, edges already browning. I read the crispening veins as a final memo: patience is just another word for staying connected after the conversation appears to end.
